speaker: interviewer
text: Of an interview with Mr. Semyon Men-- Menyuk.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they take me to the hospital that was, uh, near to the front, they ma-- they no cleaned up my wounds, and they begin to send s-- send me in the other hospital-- bigger hospitals. And they take me through-- maybe I went through six or seven hospitals--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm

speaker: interviewee
text: And they send me far away from the front. Far away-- that's in [INAUDIBLE] [? republic. ?] And in all the hospitals, I was for, uh, six, seven months, I was in hospitals. They want to take off my foot. Now, people, they was a couple times wounded, they told me, don't give it to take off the foot, because it's very easy for the-- for the doctors in-- to cut off in we young people-- and two weeks, three weeks, that's all. No, you will not have a foot. And I didn't-- I, uh, listened to the-- to the good advice from the, uh, people was-- they was a couple times wounded, and I didn't give, uh, to take off my foot. How it-- it was bad. I was-- with my foot. And it's come maybe-- and I was in the hospital until 1945. By approximately March, they send me out from the hospital on crutches. Then become a more worse problem I got. The time-- everybody from the war, they got families, most of the Ukraine people had families, somebody was waiting for-- for somebody will come from the-- from the-- from the war. I don't have to whom, where to go, and where to leave-- nothing. I remember, they gave me from the hospital, they gave me a couple of rubles for a time-- I was a partisan. Uh, in somethings they give me some pension. No-- it's nothing to do, it was a very little, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: They give us-- they give to me food, what I can go to in any restaurants, and go to eat-- some papers. No, I got-- they ask me, where you want to go? Where is your home? I don't have a home at all. I don't know where to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I take my ticket, that they gave me free-- that's everything that's government's. They gave me the ticket, I take to a place, [? Kovell. ?] That's the place where I was in the partisans, where I got some friends, I was thinking, from the partisans, where we was together. And they gave me until I-- I went in this place, no. And the time, but I was wa-- want to go-- I was with ton-- one guy, he's a Ukrainian. And I told him-- I talked with him, and he said-- I said, I don't-- I don't have where to go. He said to me, Semyon, you know what, come with me home. I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, I want to show what kind of people we have. Ukra-- some Ukrainian want-- want to kill me, some Ukrainian helped me to be alive. They-- and this guy was-- I don't know him, we was only together in hospital. And that's in-- from [? Ades keyoblest, ?] that's from state [? Odessa. ?] The-- the-- [? [SPEAKING RUSSIAN] ?]. I don't know how to compare in English, in English language.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to him, he came-- he was-- he was without one foot at all-- only with one, and crutches. We came to the village. They take me like no-- their son. And we was together with him. And I stayed in the village maybe a couple of weeks. They propose me we-- we will married you here. What, we will find a girl. How it will be, no-- I was thinking, no, I-- I want to see, see to go what happened at-- maybe something is alive, maybe something-- somebody I will find. No. And I stay with him a couple weeks. Then I was thinking, let me go. And that-- when I was leaving from the hospital. It was a lady-- she was working-- she was working in hospital, how it's-- a med-- a med--

speaker: interviewer
text: Technician?

speaker: interviewee
text: Technician, yes. And she gave me- I told her what I will through, Kiev.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: She give me address to her sister in Kiev. I say, I will be in Kiev-- no, to have enough time like this, to have others to come to stay for a one hour, it's good, too. I t-- I told her, yes, I will come to your sister. And I'll give you-- no, I'll say hello from you, and that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Kiev, and I come to-- I find her sister. And I told her what I'm [INAUDIBLE], that I was in [INAUDIBLE], and I saw your sister. Then, you know how it's-- begin to talk, begin to talk. And she said, you know what? I have a girl. I know her. She's working with my husband, [INAUDIBLE]. She's working on the railroad.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, OK. Meet me with her. And one day, she met-- we went and she-- and I met with my wife in Kiev. I tell-- told her the story-- I'm in cre-- crutch-- cre-- crutches. No, I'm young. No, I cannot work. It's very, very hard. And the first time, I remember the moment when she was working the railroad, and I told her, I will-- I will go with you. I'll-- to the railroad. And we need to go-- it's the street buses in Kiev.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And no, it's after-- it's not the d-- uh-huh. The-- the end of the war, it was the 9 of May. Now we little-- back-- we met a little earlier. No, this episode I will never forget, is she began to run to the-- to the-- to-- to the street--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the street wagon, how it's called?

speaker: interviewer
text: Street car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Street-- street cars, yes. And I cannot run.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the chauffeur, or how to call what-- she said that an invalid canno-- and she was waiting for me. And I come, come and sit, and then I-- I go with-- with her. No. And then she need to go in from the railroad-- I say, you know what, [? Mina, ?] I don't have where to go. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I will go to your home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- you know what, at the time difference, how I don't want to cry, how I don't-- it-- it comes. She tell-- she cannot say not, and she cannot say yes. No. I told her, no she. I explained to her what-- what-- what I'm not-- not, uh-- [INAUDIBLE]. It's happened in my life. But I didn't want to be like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came back by myself to-- to her home. Her-- her father, it was the same in the war, on the front-- it was only the mother, and the mother's sister, and one brother. And I talk with the mo-- with her mother. No, I take Mina to the-- I come, I don't have where to do-- where to-- where to stay, what. And she begin to ask me, do you have-- do you have anywhere a, a wife, or what? I say, I didn't got the time, but I can-- I can have a wife. No. And that she came-- Mina came back, and we begin to talk with her [? dad and ?] [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went-- and we-- you know, in the office in Russia, you-- you don't go to-- to-- to the [? rabbi ?] or what. We didn't [INAUDIBLE], and we make the papers. And I begin to live in Kiev. And Kiev, it was a terrible time. In awhile, it's past a year, it was born, my older son, in 1946, Michael. The time is very bad-- we don't have what to eat, they-- they give us where-- who works-- the government give very little bread [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was talking with the wife and telling her, what to do? We cannot stay in Kiev, why we stay in Kiev? Let me go in my place, where I was-- I was in ghetto, where I was before the war. We will see-- I went one time and I saw it's more food in these places, in the smaller villages. And I decided to go in the same city where I was in ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to my city, Kolki, and begin to work. I was working in beginning, director, a restaurant, then the director a warehouse. I was working with all my heart, and I was working it, and they give me all-- all the time, how it's called, better-- better jobs. No. In the time, they send me to study a special school for [INAUDIBLE]. It was the headquarters from-- from government business.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- and I was study-- it's in [? Livov. ?] Then I came and they give me a nice post. What I got, a state-- but I was their head from a state of government business. Jewish people at that time, it was very little [INAUDIBLE]. And one-- one guy came, by name Lipseski, he came, too, from-- he was not in the army, not-- [? they ?] was ever [? created. ?] I know a couple Russian Jewish people. No. And they know what I'm-- I'm a Jew from-- from Kolki, that I was in ghetto, no?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the time the Russian-- I cannot-- I-- if I will say what I want to do something for the-- for the Jewish people what it was killed in ghetto, they was looking at [INAUDIBLE] time in the [? least. ?] Well, you was looking-- they said everybody must be international. [INAUDIBLE] I was looking into, what is-- I'm an international, too. Well, no, the heart was-- no I cannot-- I didn't-- cannot do nothing. No. And it was-- I stay in this city for maybe, I was working 10 years, or eight years. Then they take me in another place, and another place. And no, they're short episodes-- no, in Kolki was born our other son, [? Jakob-- ?] [? Yasha. ?] Then was born in the-- a daughter, Fiena, and we got three children. And I begin to think how to go back from the-- from the small cities. The children begin to grow up, we need to teach them in bigger school, know more-- give them more education. And we decided, the wife, we'll go back to Kiev. To Kiev, it was not easy in Russia to move your place to leave. It's not what you come-- you came in a place you can rent a apartment you can live. No. I came. No, somebody helped me, too. And I came to a city that's near to Kiev, very close. A city like Brovary. There it was 19-- I think 1970, or '71, approximately. No, and we stay, and I was working in a building organization. No, we tried to work how we could, the best-- because if you will work bad, somebody [? who ?] don't like, and I-- I won't to promotion too. It's better for yourself and the-- and the children. No. Then it became a time when people begin to go out from Russia. I helped know a lot of people-- helped to go now [? in ?] Israel. No, by myself, I didn't get the time like in that time to go out-- I got afraid. Maybe who knows how it will [? be ?] children. I-- we didn't care most for ourself, I and my wife, we care for our children, how it will be. No. And I got time what to find a cousin in Canada. That was in 1973. And I-- she sent me for an invitation to come to Canada for a-- for a couple of weeks. No. I come to the government, the Russian government, and I told them that I want to go. From Russia, they don't give permission to go, at that time, to go out from-- from the country. No, I was nice-- I got work here, and nobody can tell me what I'm not-- drunk, or what I can be drunk, or what. No, and they give me permission-- 1973, I went to Canada, and I saw this life in Canada. And I talked with my relatives, and they told me-- then I see what-- nothing I can do that's in Russia, and what to do in the same that's-- [? begin ?] if we say that in Russia, after the war, it was not so much anti-Semitism. No, [? at that ?] time, past that time, and more with after-- after the-- the '50s, after-- after the '60s, there's more and more and more anti-Semitism. We decided we need to go out. And I came from Canada-- we didn't got money enough to buy the tickets we need to buy to pay for every-- every-- in the fam-- for every person in the family-- 1,000 rubles. That was a big, big money. No, when I was in Canada, I take the-- the relatives gave me some things-- clothes, what I-- I was in the flea markets. I was looking, and I bought something-- stuff, and I brought to Russia. And we sold the stuff, and we make [INAUDIBLE] the money. Then beg-- begin-- we decided to go out from Russia to Canada. Our son, the younger, the [? Dialdo ?] the son, he finished university at-- in English, Russian language. The other son, he was-- [INAUDIBLE] in that time, he started university at [INAUDIBLE] [? irrigation. ?] And he finished, and we talked with the wife let him-- we will go-- let him not finish the [?university?]. No, how-- maybe we'll need to feel better when he will finish. No, we decide he will finish. And we stayed a couple years after that, when they came from-- in the 1970-- 1976, we begin to send-- give the papers for come out from Russia. Then become the worst time in Russia-- what we leave so many years. They tell us what we-- we spied-- we're American spies, we're Israeli spies. But we leave our-- our land, no, and we go-- they told us we go to fight in Israel army. I take my sons-- they told me, you take your sons, and you-- you will fight against us in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: How we can fight against us? It was terrible. They-- they tell-- they make si-- the decision that I have military awards. They send me-- we will-- they take-- we will take your military awards, everything what you have. You're not more Russian citizen. No, that was-- I'm not [INAUDIBLE] a Russian citizen. I didn't-- that doesn't bother me. No. They take us from the jobs, they take us-- nothing to do. It only was our one-- one thing, what we-- what we got for what we buy-- food.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I am invalid from the war, I received a small pension-- military pension, which they cannot take from me. The law-- the Russian law, every single [? country, ?] they actually cannot take. Then the wife, she was working all her life a teacher. She got over 25-- the-- over five years [? awards ?] for a teacher. And one-- one man give her advice, a good man, we will give you pension. We will give you pension. She got the pension. [INAUDIBLE] the pension, we were staying in [? Kiev ?] one year.

speaker: interviewee
text: For this one year, they take me my-- my life, they take me-- not less what I spent all the years in Russia-- what I was-- [INAUDIBLE] what they want to tell me? They-- they want to-- to take me in jail. They told my wife, leave. Leave him. Leave him. Let him go. If he wants to go to Israel, let him go to Israel. And you and the children, you will stay in Russia. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Different-- different things, different things. No, we decided that was our year. And last time, I was-- I was smoking-- I was working for a night. I was-- can smoke-- I didn't sleep, I smoked maybe two packages cigare-- cigarettes. At night, one cigarette I-- no, I know what-- they will-- they can put me in jail in-- in Russia. It's not-- it's not America, and that stuff. If they want to do this, they will do it. One thing that they didn't know about me-- what I'm-- I'm invalid in the war. They didn't know. And it comes to the-- to the end where they want to tell me-- and I'm an invalid. So he said, tell me, bring me you papers where you are invalid. I brought the papers. Then they told me-- write a paper what you-- you not-- you-- it's your fault-- not-- you're not innocent. Innocent, you're [? free. ?] You're not innocent, you're--

speaker: interviewer
text: Guilty.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah. You're-- you're guilty. If you're guilty, you will have the-- tomorrow you will have in off-- in the mail a card that will give you the permit, and go from Russia. I say, OK, I'm guilty. And the ne-- next day, they're right, I have-- they send me a card, and go out from-- from the country. From the country. And-- and we met, the sons, they give the permit over-- uh, earlier. And we met with the son in [INAUDIBLE]. From the-- from [INAUDIBLE], we went-- we want to go to Canada. In Canada, they ask us to give-- what somebody can give permit for work, but-- but you will not bother the government with medicaid and med-- the government. No, we didn't got the cousin, but that was in 1973-- and the older people, they cannot give me-- no, it's not possible to come. We then got more [INAUDIBLE]. And we decide we will go to America. In America, I have relatives in Washington. And we stay maybe for one-- maybe one month or two months we was in Italy. Then they gave us the permits. We can-- we came to America. In America, when we came, we came to Washington, Silver Spring, I find some relatives. Some now--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year did you come? To America?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: '76 or, yes, '76. And all the time, that's-- no, their-- their son is-- was married in Russia with a wife. The oth-- the other son is not married yet. And the daughter is-- she finished the university in Maryland, a designer, and she's now in New York. No. And I want to, between everything, to say what the words about my now interview. That was on Su-- Sunday. Call me, my grandson, Aaron, and he knows that I will give an interview. And he said--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is Aaron?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's 15, now. He said, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN] he called me [SPEAKING RUSSIAN], it means grandfather in Russian-- [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. If you speak up, speak up everything. Don't cry, don't cry. No. If you will cry, let them go, the tears. Let them go. And I was-- I want to follow his direction. I miss a couple times, but I cry. No. Then, when we stay in America a couple of years-- I was working, I-- I will not tell all-- all the-- the-- the time what for these years, it was good, and what was bad. No. No, if to compare to this life, what we got for our-- all my life, it's here excellent, better can-- cannot be.

speaker: interviewee
text: We, one time, we sit with the wife, and we was thinking, we-- always we was talking how it was the-- the best time. We had decided to write a letter to one friend. No, that's-- this story maybe I will-- I will tell this separate. And she write us back a letter, but what-- we want to know what happened in Kolki with what-- and she write a letter, but she had read in the newspapers what it's open, a monument. A monument For the Jewish people what was killed in Kolki. And one lady, she was read a poem about me. I don't know if to know to read the-- all the poem is big, no? I will tell you short.

speaker: interviewee
text: The poem said how-- said how I was in the time in the ghetto. The time when I was jumped from the truck, how it was. I-- when we received-- when she sent the poem, we read and cry like-- like no-- I don't know what to-- for the time, what it's-- it's terrible. And then we got letters and letters from them, and they tell us what is going on. They invited us to come to Russia and to be-- Then it was-- they want to open the monument. They was looking for us, for me to bring-- to come-- to come to the Kolki, to the city. They know me very well. They know [INAUDIBLE]. They know the wife. The wife was working in the-- in the school-- in the school. She was a teacher. She was a principal, a principal, assistant principal in school what a lot of people who knows-- they begin to invite us to come to Russia for a visit to Kolki. That's what--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I want to sometimes. I will show-- I will show you it later a little. I  will show you the-- the pictures and what-- what they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. OK. So I think we're probably almost fini-- finished with the interview. [INAUDIBLE] things.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what. I mi-- I cut off a lot. I cut off because--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure you did. But is there something you really want to include that you-- that-- we can do that at the beginning of the tape and then go on to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You know what. What-- what I-- I want on the the--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Let's end this tape. [SIDE CONVERSATION]
